Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (AT), acting by shareholder Carolyn Fong

Facts.Text: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is a Singapore company whose majority shareholder and Chairman in 1996 was Peter Fong.
In 1996, Peter Fong appointed Linda Kao Chai-Chau as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

Between about 2000 and the date of the writ in Suit 477 of 2012, business opportunities from Chinese chemical companies that Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd says belonged to it were diverted away from the company.
From around 2001, further business opportunities from another Chinese company were diverted away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Around 2002, transactions forming part of a third set of business diversions affecting Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd took place.
Around 2004, additional transactions relating to the third set of business diversions affecting Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd occurred.
By 2006, Peter Fong had transferred about 51% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd to the Fong Foundation charitable trust, reducing his personal shareholding.
On 25 April 2008, Peter Fong, then Chairman of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, passed away.

In 2010, in Originating Summons No 505 of 2010, the High it was recorded for the purpose of a leave application that Peter Fong had been the controlling mind and will of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd at material times.
In 2010, shareholder Carolyn Fong obtained leave under section 216A of the Companies Act to institute a derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In 2012, Suit No 477 of 2012 was commenced as a derivative action by Carolyn Fong on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against former managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau concerning earlier business transactions.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company, acting through a shareholder in a derivative capacity, can hold a former managing director legally responsible for contravening director and management obligations by diverting business opportunities and funds away from the company in circumstances where the Chairman was said to have directed or approved the transactions.
Party_Details.Rule: A director and managing director must act in good faith in the interests of the company, avoid unauthorised diversion of corporate opportunities or assets, and may only rely on instructions or approvals from those controlling the company where such directions are within their authority and do not conflict with the company’s interests or applicable law.
Party_Details.Application: The plaintiff, acting for the company, stated that specific business opportunities and funds were diverted away from the company through transactions carried out by the managing director without proper corporate approval and contrary to the company’s interests. The managing director responded that she acted in accordance with oral instructions or an understanding with the Chairman, who was said to be the controlling mind of the company, and that the opportunities were not in truth the company’s business.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The High Court judgment in this excerpt did not determine the merits of Suit No 477 of 2012 but described it as a derivative action by shareholder Carolyn Fong on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against former managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau concerning alleged diversion of business opportunities and management obligations. The court noted that most impugned transactions occurred while Peter Fong was alive and Chairman, and that leave to commence the derivative action had been granted earlier on the basis that Peter Fong was then the controlling mind and will of the company. The present decision focused on whether Linda could pursue third party contribution or indemnity against the Estate of Peter Fong and did not resolve whether Linda ultimately committed any contravention of duties or conspiracy in relation to the derivative claim.

Party_Details.Name: Kao Chai-Chau Linda

Facts.Text: Around 1996, Linda Kao Chai-Chau was appointed managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd by its then Chairman Peter Fong.

From about 2000 onwards, Linda participated in transactions involving business opportunities from Chinese chemical companies that were later said to have been diverted away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
From around 2001, Linda was involved in further transactions relating to business opportunities from another Chinese company that were said to have been diverted from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Around 2002, Linda carried out transactions forming part of a third set of business diversions later complained of by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Around 2004, Linda undertook additional transactions connected with the third set of business diversions that Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd later challenged.

Linda maintained that in carrying out the impugned transactions she was acting on oral instructions and a standing understanding with Peter Fong regarding handling of funds and business opportunities for his friends and associates.
By the time of Peter Fong’s death on 25 April 2008, Linda had been managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd for about 12 years and continued in that role until 2012.

In 2012, Suit No 477 of 2012 was commenced as a derivative action by shareholder Carolyn Fong on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda concerning transactions mostly occurring while Peter Fong was alive.
By mid-2013, Linda’s defence position in Suit No 477 of 2012 was that all transactions complained of were carried out pursuant to Peter Fong’s specific or standing instructions or understanding.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a former managing director who carried out transactions said to divert corporate business and funds can avoid being held legally responsible to the company where she states that she was merely following the instructions or understanding of the Chairman who was treated as the controlling mind of the company.
Party_Details.Rule: A managing director owes independent duties to act in the company’s interests and cannot rely on directions from a dominant shareholder or Chairman to justify conduct that improperly diverts corporate opportunities or assets, unless such directions amount to valid corporate approval consistent with law and the company’s interests.
Party_Details.Application: Linda stated that she received and disbursed funds and business opportunities on behalf of Peter Fong’s friends and associates as a favour, in reliance on his specific and standing instructions and on his status as majority shareholder and Chairman. The company, acting through the derivative plaintiff, contended that Peter Fong either did not authorise the impugned transactions or lacked authority to authorise diversions that harmed the company, so that Linda remained independently responsible for her management decisions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: In relation to Suit No 477 of 2012, the High Court described Linda’s primary defence as being that there was no contravention of director or management obligations because the business opportunities were not those of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and she was merely following Peter Fong’s instructions as Chairman and majority shareholder. The court noted that the derivative action concerned four business opportunities, three of which were linked to Linda’s proposed contribution or indemnity claim against the Estate of Peter Fong. The judgment in this excerpt did not decide whether Linda ultimately committed any contravention of duties or conspiracy; instead, it focused on whether she could pursue third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong for contribution or indemnity in the event she was later found liable in the main action.

Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (AT), acting by Receivers and Managers

Facts.Text: On 17 January 2012, Ernst & Young was appointed as Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd pursuant to an application by shareholder Carolyn Fong.
The appointment of the Receivers and Managers was made under a consent order that reflected an apparent agreement between Carolyn Fong and Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

After their appointment, the Receivers and Managers reviewed Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd’s affairs and concluded that there was evidence of diversions of business and funds by Linda and others to the detriment of the company.
The Receivers and Managers identified that the additional diversions they were concerned with largely related to transactions or events occurring after Peter Fong’s death on 25 April 2008.
In 2012, Suit No 1015 of 2012 was commenced by the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda and 15 other individuals and corporate entities.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers claimed that Linda and 15 others engaged in a conspiracy to divert business away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd after Peter Fong’s death.
The Receivers and Managers also advanced a claim that Linda failed to comply with director and management obligations owed to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in relation to the post‑2008 transactions.

By the time Suit No 1015 of 2012 was commenced, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd’s shareholding was dispersed among the Fong Foundation and various family members, including Carolyn Fong, with Peter Fong’s personal shareholding reduced to about 3.6% before his death.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company under the control of Receivers and Managers can hold a former managing director and associated individuals legally responsible for a coordinated diversion of business and for contravening director and management obligations in respect of transactions occurring after the death of the former Chairman.
Party_Details.Rule: Persons who direct or participate in the management of a company must not engage in concerted conduct that diverts the company’s business or assets for their own benefit or that of others, and a managing director remains subject to independent duties to act in the company’s interests even where prior instructions from a former Chairman are relied upon.
Party_Details.Application: The Receivers and Managers stated that after Peter Fong’s death, Linda and multiple co‑defendants diverted business opportunities and assets away from the company through coordinated arrangements that harmed the company. Linda responded that her conduct in the later period was consistent with earlier instructions or understandings with Peter Fong and that she believed she was authorised to act as she did, while the Receivers and Managers maintained that such reliance could not justify the diversions or relieve her of her independent obligations.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment excerpt explains that Suit No 1015 of 2012 was commenced by the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda and 15 others, focusing on alleged conspiracy to divert business and on management obligations in relation to transactions largely occurring after Peter Fong’s death. The court recorded that the Receivers and Managers considered there to be evidence of diversions by Linda and others to the detriment of the company. The decision in this passage did not adjudicate on whether the conspiracy or contraventions were made out; instead, it addressed whether Linda could pursue third party contribution or indemnity against the Estate of Peter Fong in connection with both suits.

Party_Details.Name: Kao Chai-Chau Linda

Facts.Text: From about 1996 until 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau served as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
By 2006, Peter Fong had transferred the majority of his shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd to the Fong Foundation, leaving him with about 3.6% of the shares at the time of his death.
On 25 April 2008, Peter Fong, the then Chairman whose instructions Linda said she followed, passed away.
After Peter Fong’s death, Linda continued to manage Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and engaged in transactions that the Receivers and Managers later characterised as diversions of business and assets.

On 17 January 2012, Receivers and Managers were appointed over Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd following an application by shareholder Carolyn Fong, altering the control structure under which Linda had been operating.
In 2012, Suit No 1015 of 2012 was commenced by the Receivers and Managers against Linda and 15 others, focusing on events largely after Peter Fong’s death.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda was accused of participating with others in a conspiracy to divert business away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and of failing to comply with director and management obligations in relation to those transactions.

By mid‑2013, Linda’s position was that her conduct in both suits was authorised by Peter Fong’s instructions or understanding, and that she believed she was acting on behalf of his interests and those of his associates.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a managing director can be held legally responsible for post‑Chairman‑death diversions of business and assets where she maintains that her conduct was consistent with earlier instructions or understandings from the former Chairman and that she believed such conduct remained authorised.
Party_Details.Rule: A managing director’s obligations to act in the company’s interests and to avoid improper diversion of business or assets continue regardless of changes in shareholding or the death of a dominant figure, and reliance on historic instructions or understandings cannot justify later conduct that conflicts with the company’s interests or governance structure.
Party_Details.Application: The Receivers and Managers stated that after Peter Fong’s death, Linda and others diverted business away from the company and failed to comply with their obligations, causing loss to the company. Linda responded that her actions were in line with a prior pattern of instructions and understandings with Peter Fong and that she believed she remained authorised to manage funds and opportunities in that manner, while the Receivers and Managers argued that any such historic understanding could not validate the later diversions.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The High Court noted that Suit No 1015 of 2012 was brought by the Receivers and Managers against Linda and 15 others, alleging conspiracy to divert business and contraventions of director and management obligations in relation to transactions mainly after Peter Fong’s death. The judgment in this passage did not determine whether Linda was legally responsible for those alleged diversions; instead, it analysed whether she could seek contribution or indemnity from the Estate of Peter Fong under the Civil Law Act if she were later found liable. The court ultimately allowed Linda to pursue third party proceedings for contribution against the Estate, leaving the substantive liability issues in Suit No 1015 of 2012 to be decided at trial.
